The childcare infrastructure expansion requires medium-term capital investment. However, the health-sector flexible work provisions can be implemented at low cost through regulatory changes. The political feasibility is supported by the Women's Rights Center and the Parents Association (Udruzenje Roditelji), both of which have publicly advocated for preserving the 365-day primary caregiver leave.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Reforma Ravnoteže Između Posla i Privatnog Života s Fokusom na Zdravlje
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo zdravlja predlaže zadržavanje punog roditeljskog odsustva od 365 dana za primarnog staratelja, uz dodavanje 60 dana neprenosivog očinskog odsustva kao dodatnog prava, pretvaranje odsustva za njegu u plaćeni status s produženim trajanjem, te uvođenje fleksibilnih radnih odredbi specifičnih za zdravstveni sektor. Ovaj pristup čuva zaštitu zdravlja majki i djece, istovremeno podsticajući ravnopravno roditeljstvo i podržavajući porodične negovatelje osoba s zdravstvenim stanjima ili invaliditetom.
key_changes_mne: Zadržati roditeljsko odsustvo od 365 dana za primarnog staratelja i dodati 60 dana neprenosivog odsustva za drugog roditelja kao dopunsko pravo, umjesto preraspodijele postojećeg odsustva.
Pretvoriti odsustvo za njegu od 5 dana u plaćeni status i proširiti na 10 dana godišnje, uz dodatnih 10 dana za negovatelje osoba s invaliditetom ili terminalnom bolešću.

Propisati plan ulaganja u infrastrukturu za brigu o djeci s obavezujućim opštinskim ciljevima za mjesta u jaslicama za djecu do 3 godine, kao preduslov prije bilo kakvog smanjenja odsustva za primarnog staratelja.

Uspostaviti okvir fleksibilnog rada u zdravstvenom sektoru koji omogućava telezdravstvo, djelimične kliničke rasporede i podjelu radnog mjesta za zdravstvene radnike koji su roditelji ili negovatelji.
Uvesti plaćeno odsustvo za medicinsko praćenje do 5 dana godišnje za roditelje djece s hroničnim stanjima i negovatelje osoba na bolničkom liječenju.
expected_benefits_mne: Održane stope dojenja i bolji zdravstveni ishodi u ranom djetinjstvu zahvaljujući čuvanju punog 12-mjesečnog odsustva za primarnog staratelja.
Poboljšano zadržavanje zdravstvenih radnika, posebno medicinskih sestara i ljekara, kroz fleksibilne opcije rada specifične za sektor.
Snažnija podrška deinstitucionalizaciji putem smislenog plaćenog odsustva za njegu porodicama koje brinu o osobama s invaliditetom.
Smanjeni rizici po mentalno zdravlje majki povezani s prijevremenim povratkom na posao u odsustvu adekvatne brige o djeci.
Bolje usklađivanje s preporukama SZO o ishrani dojenčadi i crnogorskom strategijom javnog zdravlja.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Umjerena izvodljivost. Dodatni troškovi plaćenog odsustva za njegu i dopunskog očinskog odsustva zahtijevali bi povećana budžetska sredstva iz fonda zdravstvenog osiguranja (RFZO) i državnog budžeta, procijenjena na 2–4 miliona EUR godišnje. Proširenje infrastrukture za brigu o djeci zahtijeva kapitalna ulaganja u srednjoročnom periodu. Međutim, fleksibilne radne odredbe u zdravstvenom sektoru mogu se implementirati uz niske troškove kroz regulatorne promjene. Političku izvodljivost podržavaju Centar za ženska prava i Udruženje Roditelji, koji su oba javno zagovarali očuvanje roditeljskog odsustva od 365 dana za primarnog staratelja.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju korak u pozitivnom smjeru za javno zdravlje i zdravstveni kadar u Crnoj Gori, s važnim odredbama o odsustvu za njegu, fleksibilnom radu i transparentnosti zarada koje mogu doprinijeti poboljšanju zdravstvenih ishoda i zadržavanju zdravstvenih radnika. Međutim, efektivno smanjenje porodiljskog odsustva s 12 na 10 mjeseci bez adekvatne infrastrukture dječije zaštite izaziva zabrinutost u pogledu zdravlja male djece i dobrobiti majki, dok odredbe o odsustvu za njegu (5 neplaćenih dana) nisu dovoljne da podrže porodice koje se staraju o osobama s ozbiljnim zdravstvenim stanjima ili invaliditetom.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo zdravlja smatra ove izmjene Zakona o radu neto pozitivnim korakom za javno zdravlje i zadržavanje zdravstvenog kadra, posebno u pogledu odredbi o fleksibilnom radu i pravu na odsustvo za njegu. Međutim, preraspodjela roditeljskog odsustva kojom se efektivno skraćuje odsustvo majki na 10 mjeseci nosi rizike po zdravlje male djece s obzirom na neadekvatnu infrastrukturu dječije zaštite u Crnoj Gori, a 5-dnevno neplaćeno odsustvo za njegu nije dovoljno za porodice koje se suočavaju s ozbiljnim zdravstvenim stanjima. Ministarstvo preporučuje zadržavanje 365 dana majčinskog odsustva uz dodavanje neprenosivog očinskog odsustva kao zasebnog prava, kao i prelazak na plaćeni status odsustva za njegu.
key_concerns_mne: Efektivno smanjenje porodiljskog odsustva s 12 na 10 mjeseci nosi rizik od negativnih posljedica po stopu dojenja, mentalno zdravlje majki i rani razvoj djeteta, posebno s obzirom na to da pokrivenost vrtićima za djecu mlađu od 3 godine u Crnoj Gori iznosi svega oko 37%.

Odsustvo za njegu od 5 neplaćenih dana godišnje predstavlja minimalni EU standard i potpuno je nedovoljno za porodice koje se suočavaju s ozbiljnim zdravstvenim stanjima, invaliditetom ili podržavaju ciljeve deinstitucionalizacije.

Kulturne i ekonomske prepreke dovode do toga da mnogi očevi u Crnoj Gori neće koristiti 2-mjesečno neprenosivo odsustvo, čime nastaje stvarni nedostatak u zbrinjavanju djece bez institucionalne mreže podrške.
Izmjenama nedostaju posebne odredbe za zdravstveni kadar, čime se propušta prilika da se kroz ciljane mjere fleksibilnog rada za zdravstvene radnike odgovori na problem odliva mozgova.
Neplaćeni karakter odsustva za njegu nesrazmjerno pogađa porodice s nižim prihodima, koje su statistički sklonije suočavanju sa zdravstvenim izazovima i teretom pružanja njege.

U zakonodavnom tekstu navodno se upućuje na EU Direktivu 2019/820, koja ne postoji; ispravna referenca je Direktiva 2019/1158, što ukazuje na grešku u izradi teksta koja bi mogla stvoriti pravnu nesigurnost.
recommendations_mne: Zadržati pravo na 365 dana roditeljskog odsustva za primarnog staratelja (obično majku) i dodati 2-mjesečno neprenosivo očinsko odsustvo kao zasebno pravo, prateći model koji predlaže Centar za ženska prava, umjesto preraspodjele postojećeg odsustva.

Pretvoriti 5-dnevno odsustvo za njegu iz neplaćenog u plaćeno i produžiti ga na najmanje 10 dana godišnje za staratelje osoba s ozbiljnim zdravstvenim stanjima ili invaliditetom, uz finansiranje iz Fonda za zdravstveno osiguranje (RFZO).

Izraditi obavezan plan proširenja infrastrukture dječije zaštite s konkretnim kapacitetnim ciljevima za djecu mlađu od 3 godine, prije primjene preraspodjele roditeljskog odsustva, s ciljem obezbjeđivanja pokrivenosti od najmanje 50% u roku od 3 godine.

Uvesti posebne odredbe o fleksibilnom radu za zdravstvene radnike, uključujući mogućnosti za telehealth, rad u skraćenom radnom vremenu u kliničkim sredinama i dijeljenje radnog mjesta u Kliničkom centru Crne Gore i regionalnim bolnicama.

Pokrenuti pilot projekat transparentnosti zarada u zdravstvenom sektoru u okviru novih odredbi, s posebnim osvrtom na razlike u zaradama između medicinskih sestara i ljekara u javnozdravstvenim ustanovama.
Ispraviti referencu na EU Direktivu iz 2019/820 na 2019/1158 u zakonodavnom tekstu radi pravne jasnoće i vjerodostojnosti u okviru skrining procesa pristupanja EU.

Uvesti pravo na plaćeno odsustvo za članove porodice koji prate pacijente na ljekarskim pregledima ili tretmanima, posebno za roditelje djece s hroničnim oboljenjima i staratelje osoba koje prolaze kroz složene medicinske tretmane.

assessments.summary: This Labor Law amendment package is primarily a social and employment policy measure with no direct environmental mandate. From an environmental standpoint, the decision is largely neutral. The formalization of remote and flexible work rights carries a modest indirect potential for reducing commuting-related carbon emissions, but this benefit is incidental rather than by design, and no environmental conditionality or monitoring is attached. The decision does not advance or hinder Montenegro's pressing Chapter 27 obligations, though it does consume legislative and administrative bandwidth that remains critically needed for overdue environmental transposition work.
assessments.executive_summary: The Labor Law amendments are environmentally neutral, carrying no direct impact on Montenegro's environmental protection framework or EU Chapter 27 alignment. The sole indirect benefit lies in formalized remote work rights, which could modestly reduce transport emissions if uptake is significant. The Ministry recommends that future labor-environment policy linkages be made explicit, and that legislative priority be directed toward the severely delayed National Waste Management Plan and climate legislation.
assessments.reasoning:
The decision under review amends Montenegro's Labor Law to introduce paternity leave, non-transferable parental leave, flexible and remote work rights, wage transparency measures, and purchasing power criteria for minimum wage. These reforms implement EU Directive 2019/1158 on work-life balance and EU Directive 2019/1152 on transparent working conditions. From the perspective of the Ministry of Environment, the analysis must consider both direct and indirect environmental implications.

Direct environmental impact: There is effectively none. The amendments do not touch environmental regulation, spatial planning, waste management, biodiversity protection, coastal zone management, or any area within the Ministry of Environment's core mandate. No provisions reference environmental standards, green economy transitions, or sustainable development objectives.

Indirect environmental benefit from remote work provisions: The formalization of remote and flexible work rights (Articles pertaining to the right to request telework, flexible hours, and reduced hours for parents and caregivers) carries a modest but real potential to reduce commuting-related greenhouse gas emissions. According to IEA analysis and a PNAS study (2023), full-time remote workers can reduce personal carbon footprints by up to 54%, though hybrid arrangements yield smaller gains of 11-29%. The Carbon Trust estimates that scaled hybrid working across Europe could save millions of tonnes of CO2 annually. However, Eurofound research cautions that increased residential energy consumption (heating, cooling, equipment) can partly offset commuting savings, particularly for short commutes or those using public transport. In Montenegro's context, where car-dependent commuting is common especially in Podgorica and along the coast, the net effect is likely mildly positive but small in aggregate given the limited portion of the workforce likely to exercise these rights.

Missed opportunity for green policy integration: The Government could have used these labor reforms as an opportunity to embed environmental co-benefits more deliberately. For instance, the remote work provisions could have included requirements for employers to report on commuting-related emissions reductions, or incentives for telework hubs in smaller municipalities to reduce urban congestion and spatial pressure on Podgorica. The wage transparency and minimum wage provisions could have been linked to green job classifications or circular economy workforce development.

Chapter 27 context and legislative prioritization: Montenegro's progress on EU Chapter 27 (Environment and Climate Change) remains critically limited. The Green Home shadow report and European Commission assessments note that the National Waste Management Plan has still not been adopted, the National Energy and Climate Plan (NECP) remains delayed, and most sub-areas under Chapter 27 show slow implementation with frequent deadline extensions. While labor law reform is necessary for EU accession (Chapters 2 and 19), each legislative cycle that passes without advancing Chapter 27 transposition widens the compliance gap. The Ministry of Environment notes with concern that administrative and legislative bandwidth is finite, and urges the Government to prioritize the overdue environmental acquis with equal urgency.

EU accession coherence: The decision is commendable in advancing EU approximation in the labor domain. However, Montenegro's accession process requires parallel progress across all chapters. The European Commission's 2025 enlargement report flagged environment as one of Montenegro's weakest areas. Closing Chapter 27 requires adoption of over 200 pieces of secondary legislation. Each session devoted exclusively to non-environmental transposition, without concurrent environmental progress, risks further delay in what is already the most demanding chapter of the acquis.

In summary, this is a well-intentioned and necessary social policy reform that carries no environmental harm and a small incidental climate benefit through remote work formalization. It neither advances nor undermines the Ministry of Environment's priorities, warranting a neutral assessment.
key_concerns: No environmental dimension was integrated into the labor law amendments despite available opportunities, such as linking remote work provisions to emissions monitoring or green commuting incentives.

Montenegro's Chapter 27 transposition remains critically delayed, with the National Waste Management Plan and National Energy and Climate Plan still not adopted, while legislative bandwidth is consumed by other policy areas.
The remote work provisions lack any requirement for environmental impact tracking, meaning potential carbon savings from reduced commuting will go unmeasured and unoptimized.

Administrative and institutional capacity for environmental legislation remains understaffed and underfunded, and this decision does not address the workforce needs of environmental enforcement bodies.
recommendations: In future legislative packages, require cross-ministerial environmental screening so that labor, economic, and social reforms systematically identify and incorporate environmental co-benefits where feasible.
Prioritize adoption of the National Waste Management Plan and National Energy and Climate Plan in the next legislative cycle, with the same urgency shown for labor law EU approximation.
Amend remote work implementing regulations to include optional employer reporting on commuting emissions reductions, creating a baseline for measuring the climate co-benefits of telework policies.

Explore establishing co-working or telework hubs in secondary towns and near national park buffer zones to reduce commuting pressure on Podgorica and coastal municipalities while supporting balanced regional development.
Ensure that workforce development provisions in labor law amendments explicitly support green economy skills and circular economy job creation, aligning with EU Green Deal workforce objectives.

counter_proposal.title: Green-Integrated Labor Reform
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Environment would retain all proposed labor law amendments but supplement them with environmental integration measures. Specifically, the remote work provisions would include a voluntary employer reporting mechanism on commuting emissions avoided, and a pilot program for telework hubs in five secondary municipalities (including towns near Durmitor and Biogradska Gora national parks) would be established. The minimum wage and workforce development provisions would be expanded to reference green economy job classifications aligned with the EU taxonomy. Most critically, the legislative package would be paired with a binding Government commitment to adopt the National Waste Management Plan and submit the National Energy and Climate Plan within the same parliamentary session.
key_changes: Add voluntary employer reporting on commuting emissions avoided through remote work arrangements to the telework implementing regulations.
Launch a pilot telework hub program in five secondary municipalities to reduce urban congestion and support balanced spatial development.
Include green economy and circular economy job classifications in workforce development and minimum wage provisions.
Bundle the labor law amendments with a binding Government timeline for adopting the National Waste Management Plan and National Energy and Climate Plan within the same legislative session.
Require environmental screening of all future cross-cutting legislative packages through a standardized inter-ministerial consultation process.
expected_benefits: Measurable tracking of climate co-benefits from remote work policies, providing data for Montenegro's greenhouse gas inventory reporting.
Reduced spatial and transport pressure on Podgorica and coastal cities through decentralized telework infrastructure.
Advancement of Chapter 27 obligations in parallel with labor acquis, demonstrating comprehensive EU accession readiness.
Workforce alignment with EU Green Deal and circular economy objectives, positioning Montenegro for green transition funding.
Establishment of a precedent for environmental mainstreaming across all Government policy areas.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The core labor reforms remain unchanged, so no political opposition is introduced. The environmental add-ons are modest in scope: voluntary emissions reporting imposes no mandatory burden on employers, telework hubs can be funded through EU IPA III pre-accession funds, and bundling the waste and climate plans is a matter of political will rather than technical difficulty given that drafts already exist. The inter-ministerial screening mechanism requires only a Government decision, not new legislation.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Reforma rada integrisana sa zaštitom životne sredine
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo životne sredine zadržalo bi sve predložene izmjene zakona o radu, uz dopunu mjerama za integraciju zaštite životne sredine. Konkretno, odredbe o radu na daljinu uključivale bi dobrovoljni mehanizam izvještavanja poslodavaca o smanjenim emisijama od putovanja na posao, a uspostavio bi se i pilot program telework čvorišta u pet sekundarnih opština (uključujući gradove u blizini nacionalnih parkova Durmitor i Biogradska gora). Odredbe o minimalnoj zaradi i razvoju radne snage proširile bi se kako bi obuhvatile klasifikacije radnih mjesta u zelenoj ekonomiji usklađene s EU taksonomijom. Što je najvažnije, zakonodavni paket bio bi uparen s obavezujućom obavezom Vlade da usvoji Nacionalni plan upravljanja otpadom i podnese Nacionalni energetski i klimatski plan u okviru iste parlamentarne sjednice.
key_changes_mne: Dodati dobrovoljno izvještavanje poslodavaca o emisijama od putovanja na posao koje su izbjegnute zahvaljujući aranžmanima rada na daljinu u provedbene propise o radu na daljinu.
Pokrenuti pilot program telework čvorišta u pet sekundarnih opština radi smanjenja urbane zagušenosti i podrške uravnoteženom prostornom razvoju.
Uključiti klasifikacije radnih mjesta u zelenoj i kružnoj ekonomiji u odredbe o razvoju radne snage i minimalnoj zaradi.
Povezati izmjene zakona o radu s obavezujućim rokovima Vlade za usvajanje Nacionalnog plana upravljanja otpadom i Nacionalnog energetskog i klimatskog plana u okviru iste zakonodavne sjednice.
Uvesti obaveznu ekološku provjeru svih budućih međusektorskih zakonodavnih paketa kroz standardizovani međuresorni konsultativni proces.
expected_benefits_mne: Mjerljivo praćenje klimatskih ko-benefita politika rada na daljinu, čime se prikupljaju podaci za izvještavanje Crne Gore o inventaru gasova s efektom staklene bašte.
Smanjenje prostornog i saobraćajnog pritiska na Podgoricu i priobalne gradove kroz decentralizovanu telework infrastrukturu.
Napredovanje u ispunjavanju obaveza iz Poglavlja 27 paralelno s radnim pravnim tekovinama EU, čime se demonstrira sveobuhvatna spremnost za pristupanje EU.
Usklađivanje radne snage s ciljevima EU Zelenog dogovora i kružne ekonomije, čime se Crna Gora pozicionira za finansiranje zelene tranzicije.
Uspostavljanje presedana za integraciju zaštite životne sredine u sve oblasti vladine politike.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Osnovne reforme rada ostaju nepromijenjene, tako da ne dolazi do uvođenja političkog otpora. Ekološki dodaci su skromnog obima: dobrovoljno izvještavanje o emisijama ne nameće obavezno opterećenje poslodavcima, telework čvorišta mogu biti finansirana kroz EU IPA III pretpristupne fondove, a objedinjavanje planova za otpad i klimu predstavlja pitanje političke volje, a ne tehničkih poteškoća, s obzirom na to da nacrti već postoje. Međuresorni mehanizam provjere zahtijeva samo odluku Vlade, a ne novo zakonodavstvo.

assessments.summary_mne: Ovaj paket izmjena Zakona o radu prvenstveno je mjera socijalne i politike zapošljavanja, bez direktnog ekološkog mandata. Sa ekološkog stanovišta, odluka je uglavnom neutralna. Formalizacija prava na rad na daljinu i fleksibilan rad ima skroman indirektan potencijal za smanjenje emisija ugljika povezanih sa svakodnevnim putovanjima, ali ova korist je slučajna, a ne planirana, i nije praćena nikakvim ekološkim uslovljavanjem ili monitoringom. Odluka ne unapređuje niti ometa hitne obaveze Crne Gore u okviru Poglavlja 27, mada troši zakonodavni i administrativni kapacitet koji je i dalje kritično potreban za zakašnjeli rad na transpoziciji propisa iz oblasti životne sredine.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu su ekološki neutralne i nemaju direktan uticaj na okvir zaštite životne sredine Crne Gore niti na usklađenost sa Poglavljem 27 EU. Jedina indirektna korist leži u formalizovanim pravima na rad na daljinu, koja bi mogla skromno smanjiti emisije iz saobraćaja ukoliko dođe do značajne primjene. Ministarstvo preporučuje da buduće veze između politike rada i životne sredine budu eksplicitno utvrđene, te da se zakonodavni prioritet usmjeri prema kritično zakašnjelom Nacionalnom planu upravljanja otpadom i klimatskom zakonodavstvu.
key_concerns_mne: Nijedna ekološka dimenzija nije integrisana u izmjene zakona o radu, uprkos dostupnim prilikama, kao što su povezivanje odredbi o radu na daljinu sa praćenjem emisija ili podsticajima za zeleno putovanje na posao.

Transpozicija Poglavlja 27 u Crnoj Gori i dalje je kritično zakašnjela – Nacionalni plan upravljanja otpadom i Nacionalni energetski i klimatski plan još uvijek nisu usvojeni, dok zakonodavni kapacitet troše druge oblasti politike.

Odredbe o radu na daljinu ne sadrže nikakav zahtjev za praćenje uticaja na životnu sredinu, što znači da potencijalne uštede emisija ugljika usljed smanjenih svakodnevnih putovanja neće biti izmjerene ni optimizovane.

Administrativni i institucionalni kapaciteti za ekološko zakonodavstvo i dalje su nedovoljno kadrovski popunjeni i finansirani, a ova odluka ne rješava potrebe za radnom snagom tijela nadležnih za sprovođenje propisa o zaštiti životne sredine.
recommendations_mne: U budućim zakonodavnim paketima, uvesti obaveznu međuresornu ekološku provjeru kako bi se u radnim, ekonomskim i socijalnim reformama sistematski prepoznavale i uključivale ekološke ko-koristi gdje god je to izvodljivo.

Prioritizovati usvajanje Nacionalnog plana upravljanja otpadom i Nacionalnog energetskog i klimatskog plana u narednom zakonodavnom ciklusu, s istom hitnosti koja je pokazana za usklađivanje zakona o radu sa pravom EU.

Izmijeniti podzakonske akte o radu na daljinu kako bi se uvelo opciono izvještavanje poslodavaca o smanjenju emisija usljed putovanja na posao, čime bi se stvorila osnova za mjerenje klimatskih ko-koristi politika rada na daljinu.

Razmotriti osnivanje prostora za zajednički rad ili centara za rad na daljinu u manjim gradovima i u blizini tampon-zona nacionalnih parkova, radi smanjenja pritiska svakodnevnih putovanja na Podgoricu i primorske opštine, uz podršku uravnoteženom regionalnom razvoju.

Osigurati da odredbe o razvoju radne snage u izmjenama zakona o radu izričito podržavaju vještine zelene ekonomije i otvaranje radnih mjesta u kružnoj ekonomiji, usklađujući se sa ciljevima radne snage Evropskog zelenog dogovora.

assessments.summary: The Labor Law amendments represent a welcome step toward EU harmonization and improved worker protections that will, over time, benefit the tourism sector by making employment conditions more attractive and helping retain a domestic workforce. However, the amendments do not address the tourism sector's most acute crisis -- the lack of a permanent seasonal worker framework -- and the new obligations around paternity leave, flexible work requests, and purchasing-power-indexed minimum wages will impose additional costs on small and medium hospitality enterprises, particularly during the compressed peak season when labor shortages already exceed 8,000 workers in Budva alone. The decision is positive in direction but incomplete in addressing tourism-specific labor market dysfunction.
assessments.executive_summary: The Ministry of Tourism views these Labor Law amendments as a positive but incomplete reform. While EU directive alignment and improved worker rights will gradually make tourism employment more attractive, the amendments fail to address the sector's most critical need: a permanent seasonal worker framework. The government should fast-track the stalled Law on Permanent Seasonal Workers alongside these reforms to deliver meaningful impact for Montenegro's largest economic sector.
assessments.reasoning:
EU ALIGNMENT AND ACCESSION CONTEXT: These amendments implement EU Directive 2019/1158 (work-life balance) and EU Directive 2019/1152 (transparent working conditions), contributing to Montenegro's obligations under Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment). While not directly tied to Chapter 27 (Environment and Climate Change), which is this ministry's primary EU concern, any progress on EU accession chapters strengthens Montenegro's overall negotiating position. The December 2025 provisional closure of five additional chapters demonstrates momentum, and these labor reforms maintain that trajectory. For a tourism-dependent economy seeking EU membership, credible social policy alignment is essential -- tourism investors and international hotel chains increasingly require EU-standard labor frameworks.

IMPACT ON TOURISM LABOR MARKET: Montenegro's tourism sector faces a structural labor crisis. According to Montenegrobusiness.eu reporting, Budva alone requires over 8,000 seasonal workers, and the broader sector suffers from high turnover, wage volatility, and poor off-season employment security. The amendments' provisions on flexible work and remote work formalization could marginally help year-round tourism operations (booking management, marketing, administrative functions) retain staff, particularly in the northern municipalities where winter tourism in Kolasin and Zabljak is growing. However, the core workforce challenge in tourism is not addressed: the Law on Permanent Seasonal Workers remains stalled in legislative limbo despite repeated government announcements. This is the single most important labor reform for tourism, and its absence significantly diminishes the practical impact of these amendments on the sector.

MINIMUM WAGE AND PURCHASING POWER CRITERION: Introducing purchasing power as a criterion for minimum wage determination could incrementally raise labor costs for hospitality businesses. Montenegro's current minimum wage of EUR 670 per month is already a pressure point for small coastal restaurants, guesthouses, and tour operators operating on thin margins during a five-month season. While higher wages are desirable and help attract domestic workers over foreign seasonal labor, the mechanism must be carefully calibrated. If purchasing power adjustments outpace productivity gains in tourism, businesses may accelerate informal employment or reduce service capacity -- both outcomes that undermine tourism quality and tax revenue.

PATERNITY LEAVE AND SEASONAL PEAK: The 10-day paternity leave and non-transferable two-month parental leave minimum, while socially important, present logistical challenges for peak-season tourism operations. A chef, boat captain, or hotel manager taking leave during July-August creates significant operational disruption for small businesses that cannot easily find qualified replacements. Larger hotel chains (such as the incoming Swissotel in Kolasin) can absorb this; a family-run konoba in Kotor or a rafting operator in Tara Canyon cannot. The law should include provisions for seasonal business continuity planning or provide temporary replacement subsidies.

NORTHERN REGION IMPLICATIONS: The flexible and remote work provisions are particularly relevant for northern Montenegro's emerging tourism economy. Kolasin, Zabljak, Mojkovac, and surrounding municipalities are positioning themselves for year-round tourism (winter sports, eco-tourism, agritourism), but face severe talent retention challenges as young people migrate to Podgorica or the coast. Remote work rights could help tourism marketing, reservation management, and administrative functions be performed from northern locations year-round, supporting the government's stated goal of reducing the north-south economic disparity. This is a modest but real positive.

WAGE TRANSPARENCY AND GENDER-NEUTRAL PROVISIONS: The requirement for measures when wage gaps exceed 5% and gender-neutral job advertisements are positive signals for tourism workforce diversification. Montenegro's hospitality sector has traditionally had gendered employment patterns (women in housekeeping and reception, men in kitchen and management). Wage transparency requirements could help address this, improving the sector's attractiveness to a broader labor pool. However, enforcement capacity remains a concern -- the Labour Inspectorate's resources are stretched thin, particularly during peak season when violations are most common.

WHAT IS MISSING: From a tourism ministry perspective, these amendments are good social policy but miss the sector's structural needs. The stalled permanent seasonal worker framework, a coherent seasonal employment insurance model, training and upskilling programs for hospitality workers, and incentives for off-season employment retention are all more urgent for tourism than the provisions adopted here. The decision represents progress on EU alignment but a missed opportunity for tourism-specific labor reform.
key_concerns: The Law on Permanent Seasonal Workers remains stalled despite being the most critical labor reform for tourism -- these amendments do not address the sector's core structural employment challenge

Paternity and parental leave obligations during the compressed May-October peak season will disproportionately burden small and micro tourism enterprises (konobas, family guesthouses, tour operators) that cannot easily find qualified replacements

The purchasing power criterion for minimum wage could incrementally raise labor costs for hospitality businesses operating on thin seasonal margins, potentially pushing more employment into the informal economy
Enforcement capacity of the Labour Inspectorate is insufficient to monitor compliance across thousands of small tourism businesses, particularly during peak season when violations are most prevalent
No seasonal business continuity provisions or replacement subsidies accompany the new leave entitlements, creating an asymmetric burden on tourism versus year-round sectors
recommendations: Fast-track the stalled Law on Permanent Seasonal Workers as a companion measure to these amendments, establishing a legal framework that provides year-round social insurance coverage and employment continuity for tourism workers

Introduce a seasonal business continuity mechanism allowing micro and small tourism enterprises (under 10 employees) to access a pool of pre-qualified replacement workers or receive temporary wage subsidies when employees exercise new leave entitlements during peak season
Phase the purchasing power minimum wage criterion with a tourism sector impact assessment, ensuring adjustments account for the seasonal revenue model and do not accelerate informal employment
Strengthen Labour Inspectorate capacity with dedicated seasonal tourism compliance teams deployed to coastal and national park municipalities from May through October

Link these labor reforms to the northern region development strategy by creating remote work incentives for tourism companies that establish year-round administrative operations in Kolasin, Zabljak, or other northern municipalities

counter_proposal.title: Integrated Tourism Labor Reform Package
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than adopting general labor law amendments in isolation, the Ministry of Tourism would bundle these EU-mandated reforms with the long-stalled permanent seasonal worker framework into a single comprehensive tourism labor reform package. This would address both EU compliance requirements and the sector's structural employment crisis simultaneously, ensuring that new worker protections are accompanied by mechanisms that make them practically viable for tourism's unique seasonal operating model.
key_changes: Bundle Labor Law amendments with the Law on Permanent Seasonal Workers into a single legislative package with a unified implementation timeline
Create a Tourism Employment Continuity Fund financed by a small levy on peak-season accommodation revenue to subsidize leave replacement costs for micro-enterprises
Establish a seasonal worker registry and training program allowing pre-qualified replacements to be deployed when permanent staff exercise parental or flexible work rights
Introduce graduated compliance timelines -- large hotel chains comply immediately, small and micro tourism businesses receive an 18-month transition period with technical assistance
Mandate that the purchasing power minimum wage criterion include a tourism sector advisory panel with employer and worker representation before any adjustment takes effect
expected_benefits: Simultaneous resolution of EU compliance requirements and the tourism sector's structural seasonal employment crisis
Reduced operational disruption for small tourism businesses during peak season through replacement worker access and phased implementation
Stronger worker retention in tourism by combining new rights with year-round employment security through the permanent seasonal worker framework
More balanced north-south development by linking remote work incentives to northern tourism administrative operations
Credible enforcement through graduated compliance and strengthened seasonal inspection capacity
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: Moderately feasible. The permanent seasonal worker law has already been drafted and consulted upon -- the political will exists but bureaucratic inertia has delayed it. Bundling it with EU-mandated reforms creates stronger political urgency. The Tourism Employment Continuity Fund would require new legislation but could be modeled on similar Mediterranean schemes (Croatia's seasonal employment support). Graduated compliance timelines are standard EU practice for SME accommodation. The main obstacle is inter-ministerial coordination between the Ministry of Labor and tourism stakeholders, which has historically been weak.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Integrisani paket reformi radnog zakonodavstva u turizmu
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto usvajanja opštih izmjena zakona o radu izolovano, Ministarstvo turizma bi objedinilo ove reformе naložene od strane EU sa dugo odlaganim okvirom za stalne sezonske radnike u jedan sveobuhvatan paket reformi radnog zakonodavstva u turizmu. Na taj način bi se istovremeno odgovorilo na zahtjeve usklađenosti s EU i na strukturnu krizu zapošljavanja u sektoru, osiguravajući da nova prava radnika budu praćena mehanizmima koji ih čine praktično ostvarivim u okviru jedinstvenog sezonskog modela poslovanja u turizmu.
key_changes_mne: Objediniti izmjene Zakona o radu sa Zakonom o stalnim sezonskim radnicima u jedan zakonodavni paket sa jedinstvenim rokom implementacije
Osnovati Fond za kontinuitet zapošljavanja u turizmu, finansiran malim nametom na prihode od smještaja u sezoni vršnog opterećenja, radi sufinansiranja troškova zamjene za odsustvo u mikropreduzećima

Uspostaviti registar sezonskih radnika i program obuke koji bi omogućio raspoređivanje prethodno kvalifikovanih zamjena kada stalni zaposleni ostvaruju pravo na roditeljsko odsustvo ili fleksibilno radno vrijeme
Uvesti postepene rokove usklađenosti -- veliki hotelski lanci ispunjavaju obaveze odmah, dok mala i mikro turistička preduzeća dobijaju prijelazni period od 18 mjeseci uz tehničku podršku
Obavezati da kriterijum minimalne zarade prema kupovnoj moći uključuje savjetodavni panel turističkog sektora sa predstavnicima poslodavaca i radnika prije stupanja na snagu bilo kakve izmjene
expected_benefits_mne: Istovremeno rješavanje zahtjeva usklađenosti s EU i strukturne krize sezonskog zapošljavanja u turističkom sektoru
Smanjenje operativnih poremećaja za mala turistička preduzeća tokom sezone vršnog opterećenja kroz pristup zamjenskim radnicima i postepenu implementaciju
Bolje zadržavanje radnika u turizmu kombinovanjem novih prava sa sigurnošću zaposlenja tokom cijele godine kroz okvir za stalne sezonske radnike
Uravnoteženiji razvoj sjevera i juga kroz povezivanje podsticaja za rad na daljinu sa administrativnim operacijama sjevernog turizma
Vjerodostojna primjena kroz postepenu usklađenost i ojačane kapacitete sezonske inspekcije
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Umjereno ostvarivo. Zakon o stalnim sezonskim radnicima je već izrađen i prošao je konsultacije -- politička volja postoji, ali birokratska inercija ga je odložila. Njegovo objedinjavanje sa reformama nametnutim od strane EU stvara snažniji politički pritisak. Fond za kontinuitet zapošljavanja u turizmu zahtijevao bi novo zakonodavstvo, ali bi se mogao modelovati prema sličnim mediteranskim shemama (hrvatska podrška sezonskom zapošljavanju). Postepeni rokovi usklađenosti su standardna praksa EU za smještajne kapacitete malih i srednjih preduzeća. Glavna prepreka je međuministarska koordinacija između Ministarstva rada i turističkih aktera, koja je istorijski bila slaba.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju pozitivan korak ka usklađivanju s EU i unapređenju zaštite radnika, što će vremenom koristiti turističkom sektoru čineći uslove zapošljavanja atraktivnijim i pomažući zadržavanju domaće radne snage. Međutim, izmjene ne rješavaju najurgentniiju krizu turističkog sektora -- odsustvo trajnog okvira za sezonske radnike -- a nove obaveze u pogledu porodiljskog odsustva za očeve, zahtjeva za fleksibilnim radom i minimalne zarade indeksirane kupovnom moći nametaće dodatne troškove malim i srednjim ugostiteljskim preduzećima, posebno tokom zbijene ljetnje sezone kada manjak radne snage već premašuje 8.000 radnika samo u Budvi. Odluka je pozitivna po smjeru, ali nepotpuna u rješavanju disfunkcionalnosti tržišta rada specifičnih za turizam.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo turizma smatra ove izmjene Zakona o radu pozitivnom, ali nepotpunom reformom. Iako će usklađivanje s direktivama EU i unapređenje prava radnika postepeno učiniti zapošljavanje u turizmu atraktivnijim, izmjenama se ne rješava najkritičnija potreba sektora: trajni okvir za sezonske radnike. Vlada bi trebalo da paralelno s ovim reformama ubrza proceduru za blokirani Zakon o stalnim sezonskim radnicima, kako bi se ostvario stvarni uticaj na najveći ekonomski sektor Crne Gore.
key_concerns_mne: Zakon o stalnim sezonskim radnicima i dalje je blokiran, uprkos tome što predstavlja najkritičniju reformu rada za turizam -- ove izmjene ne rješavaju temeljni strukturalni izazov zapošljavanja u sektoru

Obaveze u vezi s porodiljskim odsustvom za očeve i roditeljskim odsustvom tokom zbijene sezone od maja do oktobra nesrazmjerno će opteretiti mala i mikro turistička preduzeća (konobe, porodične pansione, turoperatore) koja ne mogu lako pronaći kvalifikovane zamjene

Kriterijum kupovne moći za minimalnu zaradu mogao bi postepeno povećati troškove rada za ugostiteljske firme koje posluju s uskim sezonskim maržama, potencijalno gurajući sve više zapošljavanja u sivu ekonomiju
Kapacitet Inspektorata rada nedovoljan je za praćenje usklađenosti u hiljadama malih turističkih preduzeća, posebno tokom sezone kada su prekršaji najčešći

Novim pravima na odsustvo ne prate nikakve odredbe o kontinuitetu poslovanja za sezonska preduzeća niti subvencije za zamjenu radnika, što stvara asimetrično opterećenje za turizam u odnosu na sektore koji rade tokom cijele godine
recommendations_mne: Ubrzati proceduru za blokirani Zakon o stalnim sezonskim radnicima kao prateću mjeru uz ove izmjene, uspostavljajući pravni okvir koji osigurava cjelogodišnje pokriće socijalnim osiguranjem i kontinuitet zapošljavanja turističkih radnika

Uvesti mehanizam za obezbjeđivanje kontinuiteta poslovanja sezonskih preduzeća koji bi mikro i malim turističkim preduzećima (do 10 zaposlenih) omogućio pristup skupini unaprijed kvalifikovanih zamjenskih radnika ili privremenim subvencijama za zarade kada zaposleni koriste nova prava na odsustvo tokom sezone

Postepeno uvoditi kriterijum kupovne moći za minimalnu zaradu uz prethodnu procjenu uticaja na turistički sektor, osiguravajući da korekcije uzimaju u obzir sezonski model prihoda i da ne ubrzavaju rast neformalnog zapošljavanja
Ojačati kapacitete Inspektorata rada formiranjem namjenskih timova za nadzor usklađenosti u turističkoj sezoni, raspoređenih u primorskim opštinama i opštinama nacionalnih parkova od maja do oktobra

Povezati ove reforme rada sa strategijom razvoja sjeverne regije stvaranjem podsticaja za rad na daljinu za turistička preduzeća koja uspostave cjelogodišnje administrativno poslovanje u Kolašinu, Žabljaku ili drugim opštinama na sjeveru

assessments.summary: The Labor Law amendments are a welcome step toward EU harmonization and improved working conditions, directly supporting Montenegro's accession trajectory under Chapters 19 and 23. From the Ministry of Interior's perspective, the reforms are broadly positive but raise operational concerns regarding their application to law enforcement, border police, and civil protection personnel, where 24/7 operational readiness is non-negotiable. The absence of explicit sector-specific adaptations for security services creates potential scheduling, staffing, and readiness risks that must be addressed through secondary legislation or collective agreements before implementation.
assessments.executive_summary: The Labor Law amendments positively advance EU alignment, but the Ministry of Interior flags the lack of explicit operational adaptations for law enforcement and emergency services as a significant gap. The key recommendation is to develop sector-specific implementing regulations that reconcile new parental and flexible work rights with the 24/7 operational demands of policing, border management, and civil protection before the amendments take effect.
assessments.reasoning:
From the Ministry of Interior's standpoint, this decision must be evaluated across several dimensions: EU accession compliance, operational impact on security services, administrative capacity, and workforce management within the Uprava policije and broader interior security apparatus.

EU Accession Alignment (Positive): The amendments implement EU Directive 2019/1158 on work-life balance and EU Directive 2019/1152 on transparent working conditions. This harmonization directly supports Montenegro's EU accession process. Montenegro received a positive Interim Benchmark Assessment Report (IBAR) for Chapters 23 and 24 in June 2024, and demonstrating continued legislative alignment strengthens the country's position as it works toward closing benchmarks. From a Chapter 24 (Justice, Freedom, and Security) perspective, a modern, EU-compliant labor framework improves Montenegro's institutional credibility. As reported by the European Commission's 2025 Montenegro Report, legislative alignment on social policy standards complements the rule-of-law reforms central to interior affairs.

Operational Readiness Concerns (Significant): The most pressing concern is how flexible work arrangements, including remote work and reduced hours for parents and caregivers, will apply to operational police and security personnel. Montenegro's Police Directorate operates on 24/7 shift rotations across patrol units, border crossings, criminal investigation, and special operations. The right to request flexible or remote work, while appropriate for administrative staff, cannot be applied uniformly to frontline officers without creating coverage gaps. The EU Directive 2019/1158 itself recognizes in its recitals that member states may adapt implementation for sectors where continuous service is required, but the Montenegrin amendments as described do not appear to include explicit derogations or adaptation mechanisms for the security sector. This omission could lead to legal uncertainty if police officers invoke these rights, or alternatively, to grievances if requests are systematically denied without a clear legal basis.

Staffing and Personnel Impact (Moderate Concern): Montenegro already has one of the highest police-to-population ratios in Europe (approximately one officer per 141 inhabitants according to Wikipedia data on the Police Directorate), but the OSCE and SIGMA monitoring reports have repeatedly noted that the Uprava policije suffers from inefficient distribution of personnel, excessive administrative positions relative to operational roles, and delayed recruitment. Introducing mandatory 10-day paternity leave and a non-transferable two-month parental leave minimum will require advance workforce planning to maintain minimum staffing levels, particularly at border crossing points (critical for Schengen preparation) and in specialized units such as organized crime and counter-terrorism. Without proactive planning, these absences could compound existing personnel distribution problems.

Administrative Capacity (Concern): The SIGMA 2024 monitoring report on Montenegro's public administration highlighted that administrative capacities remain weak due to delayed recruitments, high numbers of acting positions, and temporary assignments. Adding new compliance obligations, such as wage gap monitoring, gender-neutral job advertisements, and processing flexible work requests, increases the administrative burden on HR departments across interior institutions. The Ministry of Interior's own Directorate for Strategic Development, which already bears outsized responsibility for police HR planning, would need additional capacity or procedural support to manage these requirements effectively.

Wage Transparency and Minimum Wage (Minor Positive): The introduction of purchasing power as a criterion for minimum wage determination and mandatory action when wage differences exceed 5% without objective justification are broadly positive measures. For the interior sector, wage transparency could help address longstanding complaints about pay disparities between police ranks and between operational and administrative staff. However, implementation will require clear guidelines to account for legitimate differentials such as hazard pay, shift premiums, and specialized duty allowances that are standard in law enforcement compensation.

Civil Protection Implications: The five-day unpaid carer's leave provision is reasonable but must be managed carefully in civil protection contexts. During natural disasters, floods (which are recurrent in Montenegro), or other emergencies, civil protection personnel cannot be absent on short notice. Emergency recall provisions must be explicitly preserved.

Overall, the decision is sound social policy and advances EU integration. However, the Ministry of Interior requires that implementing regulations address the specific operational needs of security services. This is not opposition to the reforms but a call for the kind of sector-specific calibration that EU member states routinely apply when transposing these directives.
key_concerns: No explicit operational derogation or adaptation mechanism for law enforcement, border police, and civil protection personnel regarding flexible and remote work rights

Risk of staffing gaps at border crossing points and in 24/7 police units during simultaneous parental leave absences, particularly given existing personnel distribution inefficiencies noted by OSCE and SIGMA
Increased administrative burden on interior institutions' HR departments, which already face capacity constraints as noted in SIGMA 2024 monitoring report
Legal uncertainty about how to handle flexible work requests from frontline police officers without a clear legal framework for justified refusal
Potential complications for civil protection readiness if carer's leave rights are not balanced against emergency recall obligations
Wage transparency requirements may create friction if not properly calibrated for legitimate law enforcement pay differentials such as hazard pay and shift premiums
recommendations: Develop a Ministry of Interior implementing regulation (pravilnik) that specifies how flexible work, remote work, and parental leave rights apply to different categories of interior personnel: operational police, border police, criminal investigators, administrative staff, and civil protection officers

Establish a workforce planning protocol within the Uprava policije that ensures minimum staffing thresholds are maintained at all border crossing points and operational units when parental leave absences occur, with at least 90-day advance scheduling requirements

Coordinate with the Ministry of Labor to include explicit language in the final law or accompanying bylaws recognizing that operational security roles may have justified grounds for limiting or scheduling flexible work arrangements, consistent with EU Directive 2019/1158 provisions
Invest in the HR information systems of the Police Directorate to automate leave tracking, flexible work request processing, and wage gap monitoring, reducing the manual administrative burden

Use the wage transparency provisions as an opportunity to conduct a comprehensive pay equity audit within the interior sector, addressing longstanding disparities while establishing clear, objective criteria for legitimate differentials

Include emergency recall clauses in any implementing regulation, ensuring that carer's leave and flexible work arrangements can be temporarily suspended during declared emergencies or civil protection activations

counter_proposal.title: Sector-Adapted Implementation Framework for Interior Services
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than opposing the Labor Law amendments, the Ministry of Interior would propose a parallel adoption of a sector-specific implementing regulation that translates the new rights into operationally viable arrangements for security personnel. This framework would categorize interior employees into tiers based on operational necessity (frontline operational, investigative, administrative support, and civil protection), with differentiated modalities for exercising flexible work and leave rights. Frontline and border police would receive equivalent time-off benefits but through structured scheduling rather than on-demand flexibility, while administrative staff would have broader access to remote and flexible work options. The framework would include mandatory advance planning periods, minimum unit staffing floors, and emergency override provisions.
key_changes: Create a four-tier classification of interior personnel (operational, investigative, administrative, civil protection) with differentiated flexible work modalities for each tier
Establish 90-day advance notification requirement for parental leave in operational police units to enable replacement planning
Permit structured schedule-based flexibility for shift workers rather than ad-hoc remote work requests, ensuring equivalent work-life balance benefits
Mandate minimum staffing thresholds at each border crossing point and police station that cannot be breached by simultaneous leave approvals
Develop a digital leave management and workforce planning system integrated with existing police dispatch and scheduling systems
Create an inter-ministerial coordination mechanism with the Ministry of Labor for handling appeals or disputes about flexible work denials in the security sector
expected_benefits: Maintains full compliance with EU Directive 2019/1158 while preserving operational security readiness
Prevents ad-hoc staffing crises at border crossings during Schengen preparation phase
Provides legal certainty for both police commanders and officers regarding their rights and obligations under the new law
Modernizes police HR management through digital workforce planning tools
Positions Montenegro favorably in EU accession evaluations by demonstrating sophisticated sector-specific transposition rather than blanket application
Improves police officer morale by providing genuine work-life balance benefits adapted to the realities of shift-based security work
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High. Sector-specific implementing regulations are standard practice in EU member states when transposing work-life balance directives for security services. The regulatory framework can be developed within 3-6 months by the Ministry of Interior legal department in coordination with the Ministry of Labor, using Croatian and Slovenian models as references. The digital HR system investment would require approximately 12-18 months and EU pre-accession funding (IPA III) could cover development costs.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Sektorski Prilagođen Okvir za Implementaciju u Oblasti Unutrašnjih Poslova
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto da se suprotstavlja izmjenama Zakona o radu, Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova bi predložilo paralelno usvajanje sektorski specifičnog provedbenog propisa koji nova prava prevodi u operativno održive aranžmane za bezbjednosno osoblje. Ovaj okvir bi kategorizovao zaposlene u MUP-u u nivoe na osnovu operativne nužnosti (operativno osoblje na prvoj liniji, istražitelji, administrativna podrška i civilna zaštita), s diferenciranim modalitetima za ostvarivanje prava na fleksibilan rad i odsustvo. Policajci na prvoj liniji i granična policija dobijali bi jednake beneficije slobodnih dana, ali kroz strukturirano raspoređivanje umjesto fleksibilnosti na zahtjev, dok bi administrativno osoblje imalo širi pristup radu na daljinu i fleksibilnom radu. Okvir bi uključivao obavezne periode planiranja unaprijed, minimalne pragove popunjenosti jedinica i odredbe o vanrednom prekoračenju.
key_changes_mne: Uspostavljanje četvorostepene klasifikacije osoblja MUP-a (operativno, istražiteljsko, administrativno, civilna zaštita) s diferenciranim modalitetima fleksibilnog rada za svaki nivo
Uvođenje obaveze obavještavanja od 90 dana unaprijed za roditeljsko odsustvo u operativnim policijskim jedinicama radi omogućavanja planiranja zamjene
Omogućavanje strukturirane fleksibilnosti zasnovane na rasporedu za smjenare umjesto ad-hoc zahtjeva za rad na daljinu, uz osiguranje jednakih beneficija ravnoteže između posla i privatnog života
Utvrđivanje minimalnih pragova popunjenosti na svakom graničnom prelazu i u svakoj policijskoj stanici koji se ne mogu narušiti istovremenim odobravanjem odsustva
Razvoj digitalnog sistema za upravljanje odsustvima i planiranje radne snage, integrisanog s postojećim policijskim sistemima za dežurstva i raspoređivanje
Uspostavljanje međuresornog koordinacionog mehanizma s Ministarstvom rada za rješavanje žalbi ili sporova u vezi s odbijanjem fleksibilnog rada u bezbjednosnom sektoru
expected_benefits_mne: Osigurava potpunu usklađenost s EU Direktivom 2019/1158 uz očuvanje operativne bezbjednosne spremnosti
Sprječava ad-hoc kadrovske krize na graničnim prelazima tokom faze pripreme za Šengen
Pruža pravnu sigurnost kako policijskim komandirima tako i službenicima u pogledu njihovih prava i obaveza prema novom zakonu
Modernizuje upravljanje ljudskim resursima u policiji putem digitalnih alata za planiranje radne snage
Pozicionira Crnu Goru povoljno u ocjenama EU integracija, demonstrirajući sofisticiranu, sektorski specifičnu transpoziciju umjesto paušalne primjene
Poboljšava moral policijskih službenika pružanjem stvarnih beneficija ravnoteže između posla i privatnog života, prilagođenih realnostima smjenskog bezbjednosnog rada
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka. Sektorski specifični provedbeni propisi su standardna praksa u državama članicama EU prilikom transponovanja direktiva o ravnoteži između posla i privatnog života za bezbjednosne službe. Regulatorni okvir može biti izrađen u roku od 3 do 6 mjeseci od strane pravnog odjeljenja Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova u koordinaciji s Ministarstvom rada, uz korišćenje hrvatskog i slovenačkog modela kao referenci. Ulaganje u digitalni HR sistem zahtijevalo bi otprilike 12 do 18 mjeseci, a troškove razvoja moglo bi pokriti EU pretpristupno finansiranje (IPA III).

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju dobrodošao korak ka harmonizaciji s EU i unapređenju radnih uslova, direktno podržavajući trajektoriju pristupanja Crne Gore u okviru Poglavlja 19 i 23. Sa stanovišta Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova, reforme su u cjelini pozitivne, ali postavljaju operativna pitanja u pogledu njihove primjene na zaposlene u organima za sprovođenje zakona, graničnoj policiji i civilnoj zaštiti, gdje je operativna spremnost 24/7 neupitna. Odsustvo izričitih sektorski specifičnih prilagođavanja za bezbjednosne službe stvara potencijalne rizike po raspoređivanje, popunjavanje radnih mjesta i operativnu spremnost, koji se moraju otkloniti kroz podzakonske akte ili kolektivne ugovore prije stupanja zakona na snagu.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu pozitivno doprinose usklađivanju s EU, ali Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova ukazuje na nedostatak izričitih operativnih prilagođavanja za organe za sprovođenje zakona i hitne službe kao na značajan propust. Ključna preporuka je da se izrade sektorski specifični provedbeni propisi kojima će se uskladiti nova prava na roditeljsko i fleksibilno radno vrijeme s operativnim zahtjevima policije, upravljanja granicom i civilne zaštite koji podrazumijevaju neprekidan rad, i to prije nego što izmjene stupe na snagu.
key_concerns_mne: Ne postoji izričita operativna derogacija niti mehanizam prilagođavanja za zaposlene u organima za sprovođenje zakona, graničnoj policiji i civilnoj zaštiti u pogledu prava na fleksibilan i rad na daljinu

Rizik od manjka kadra na graničnim prelazima i u policijskim jedinicama sa neprekidnim radom usljed istovremenih odsustva po osnovu roditeljskog odsustva, naročito s obzirom na postojeće neravnomjernosti u raspoređivanju osoblja koje su zabilježili OEBS i SIGMA
Povećano administrativno opterećenje za kadrovske službe institucija unutrašnjih poslova, koje već sada imaju ograničene kapacitete, kako je navedeno u SIGMA izvještaju o praćenju za 2024. godinu
Pravna nesigurnost u pogledu postupanja po zahtjevima policijskih službenika na terenu za fleksibilan rad, bez jasnog pravnog okvira koji bi omogućio obrazloženo odbijanje
Moguće komplikacije po operativnu spremnost civilne zaštite ukoliko prava na odsustvo negovatelja ne budu uravnotežena s obavezama hitnog povratka na dužnost

Zahtjevi transparentnosti plata mogu izazvati trvenja ukoliko ne budu precizno prilagođeni legitimnim razlikama u naknadama zaposlenih u organima za sprovođenje zakona, kao što su naknade za rad u otežanim uslovima i dodaci za smjenski rad
recommendations_mne: Izraditi pravilnik Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova kojim se propisuje način primjene prava na fleksibilan rad, rad na daljinu i roditeljsko odsustvo na različite kategorije zaposlenih u unutrašnjim poslovima: operativne policajce, graničnu policiju, kriminalističke istražitelje, administrativno osoblje i službenike civilne zaštite

Uspostaviti protokol planiranja radne snage unutar Uprave policije kojim se osigurava održavanje minimalnih kadrovskih pragova na svim graničnim prelazima i u operativnim jedinicama u slučaju odsustva po osnovu roditeljskog odsustva, uz obavezno planiranje rasporeda s najmanje 90 dana unaprijed

Koordinirati s Ministarstvom rada radi unošenja izričitih odredbi u konačni tekst zakona ili prateće podzakonske akte, kojima se prepoznaje da operativne bezbjednosne funkcije mogu imati opravdane osnove za ograničavanje ili zakazivanje fleksibilnih radnih aranžmana, u skladu s odredbama Direktive EU 2019/1158

Uložiti u informacione sisteme za upravljanje ljudskim resursima Uprave policije radi automatizacije praćenja odsustva, obrade zahtjeva za fleksibilan rad i praćenja razlika u platama, čime bi se smanjilo ručno administrativno opterećenje

Iskoristiti odredbe o transparentnosti plata kao priliku za sprovođenje sveobuhvatne revizije jednakosti plata unutar sektora unutrašnjih poslova, uz otklanjanje dugogodišnjih dispariteta i uspostavljanje jasnih, objektivnih kriterijuma za legitimne razlike

Uključiti klauzule o hitnom povratku na dužnost u svaki provedbeni propis, osiguravajući da se odsustvo negovatelja i aranžmani fleksibilnog rada mogu privremeno obustaviti za vrijeme proglašenih vanrednih stanja ili aktiviranja civilne zaštite

assessments.summary: The Labor Law amendments represent a constitutionally sound and EU-aligned reform package that advances fundamental rights protections, particularly gender equality and work-life balance. The transposition of EU Directives 2019/1158 and 2019/1152 strengthens Montenegro's Chapter 23 credentials on fundamental rights and signals genuine legislative progress on the EU accession path. However, the Ministry of Justice identifies significant concerns regarding enforceability, the absence of robust judicial remedy mechanisms for new rights, and an apparent legislative reference error (citing "Directive 2019/820" rather than 2019/1158), which must be corrected before parliamentary adoption to ensure legal certainty.
assessments.executive_summary: The amendments are a positive step for fundamental rights and EU alignment, correctly transposing key work-life balance and transparent working conditions directives. The top concerns are weak enforcement infrastructure (Montenegro's labor inspectorate rarely initiates sanctions), missing judicial remedy provisions for employees whose new rights are denied, and a legislative citation error that could create legal uncertainty. The Ministry of Justice recommends coupling these substantive rights with explicit enforcement mechanisms, including dedicated labor court procedures and strengthened inspection powers.
assessments.reasoning:
CONSTITUTIONAL COMPLIANCE: The amendments are well-grounded in Montenegro's constitutional framework. Article 73 of the Constitution guarantees the right to work and prohibits forced labor. Article 8 enshrines gender equality as a constitutional value, and Article 18 prohibits direct and indirect discrimination on any grounds. The introduction of paternity leave, non-transferable parental leave, and gender-neutral job titles directly advances these constitutional guarantees. The wage transparency measures requiring justification when pay differences exceed 5% respond to documented gender pay gaps of approximately 20% (per EEAS Gender Equality Profile of Montenegro), making this a necessary corrective measure rather than legislative overreach.

EU LEGAL HARMONIZATION: This is the strongest dimension of the decision. EU Directive 2019/1158 on work-life balance requires at least 10 working days of paternity leave compensated at sick-pay level, two months of non-transferable parental leave out of the total four-month entitlement, five days of carers leave per year, and the right to request flexible working arrangements. The Montenegrin amendments track these requirements closely. Directive 2019/1152 on transparent and predictable working conditions caps probationary periods at six months for fixed-term contracts, which the amendments also incorporate. This legislative alignment is critical for Montenegro's EU accession negotiations, particularly as the European Commission's 2025 Progress Report noted a slowdown in legislative EU alignment on Chapters 23 and 24 due to domestic political challenges. However, the decision text references "EU Directive 2019/820" which does not exist. This appears to be a clerical error for Directive 2019/1158. While seemingly minor, incorrect legislative citations in a law create legal uncertainty and could complicate future judicial interpretation or EU scrutiny of transposition completeness. This must be corrected in the parliamentary draft.

RULE OF LAW AND ENFORCEABILITY: This is the Ministry of Justice's primary concern. Montenegro's labor inspection system, while structurally adequate for the formal economy according to ILO assessments, suffers from a well-documented pattern of passive enforcement. The US State Department's human rights reports note that inspectors "had the authority to make unannounced inspections and initiate sanctions, but rarely did so." The 2023 inspection data showed 6,435 inspections, but the government failed to report 2024 figures, suggesting deteriorating transparency. New rights such as the right to request flexible work, protection from retaliation for declining remote work, and wage transparency obligations are only meaningful if employees can enforce them without fear of reprisal. The amendments, as described, do not appear to include: (a) explicit penalties for employer non-compliance with the new provisions; (b) a clear burden-of-proof mechanism for discrimination claims related to these new rights (EU Directive 2019/1158, Article 12 requires that the burden of proof shifts to the employer in cases of alleged victimization); (c) protection against dismissal or adverse treatment for exercising these rights, with specific remedies; (d) a streamlined judicial procedure for work-life balance disputes, given Montenegro's severe court backlog (the Administrative Court's efficiency dropped 89% per the latest CEPEJ report, and average case resolution stands at 739 days).

HUMAN RIGHTS IMPACT: The decision advances multiple human rights dimensions. The non-transferable parental leave for fathers challenges entrenched gender stereotypes and promotes substantive equality, consistent with ECHR case law on non-discrimination (Article 14 read with Article 8, right to family life). The carers leave provision recognizes the disproportionate burden of informal care on women. Wage transparency measures address structural discrimination. Gender-neutral job advertisements promote equal access to employment. These are all consistent with Montenegro's obligations under CEDAW and the European Social Charter (revised). The Women's Rights Center (Centar za zenska prava) has publicly stated that "the reform needs to be aligned with system capacities and the interests of children and parents," signaling concern about implementation readiness rather than substantive objections.

ANTI-CORRUPTION DIMENSION: The wage transparency provisions have an indirect but important anti-corruption benefit. By requiring employers to justify pay differentials exceeding 5%, the amendments reduce scope for arbitrary, nepotistic, or politically-motivated compensation practices, which are documented concerns in Montenegro's public and private sectors. However, without specifying which body monitors compliance (the Agency for Prevention of Corruption, the labor inspectorate, or an ombudsperson), this provision risks becoming declaratory rather than operative.

JUDICIAL SYSTEM IMPLICATIONS: Montenegro's judiciary is already severely overburdened. The Supreme Court backlog stands at 93% (highest in Europe), and the introduction of new enforceable rights will inevitably increase litigation. Without parallel investment in labor court capacity or alternative dispute resolution mechanisms, the new rights risk being undermined by practical inaccessibility of judicial remedies. This concern is directly relevant to Chapter 23 closing benchmarks, which require demonstrated judicial efficiency alongside substantive rights protection.
key_concerns: The decision text cites 'EU Directive 2019/820' which does not exist; this is likely a reference to Directive 2019/1158 and must be corrected before parliamentary adoption to ensure legal certainty and proper transposition documentation.

Montenegro's labor inspectorate has a documented pattern of passive enforcement, rarely initiating sanctions despite having authority to do so, which threatens the practical effectiveness of the new employee rights.
The amendments do not appear to include explicit burden-of-proof provisions shifting to the employer in victimization cases, as required by Article 12 of EU Directive 2019/1158.

Montenegro's severe judicial backlog (739-day average case resolution, 93% Supreme Court backlog) makes judicial enforcement of these new rights practically inaccessible without parallel court capacity investment.
The wage transparency monitoring mechanism lacks clarity on which institution (labor inspectorate, Agency for Prevention of Corruption, or other body) is responsible for oversight and enforcement.
The Women's Rights Center has flagged concerns about alignment with system capacities, suggesting the reform may outpace institutional readiness for implementation.
recommendations: Correct the legislative citation from 'Directive 2019/820' to 'Directive 2019/1158' before the draft reaches parliamentary committee review.

Include explicit burden-of-proof provisions requiring the employer to demonstrate that adverse treatment was unrelated to an employee's exercise of work-life balance rights, in compliance with Article 12 of Directive 2019/1158.
Establish a graduated sanctions regime for employer non-compliance, with specific fine ranges for violations of paternity leave, flexible work, and wage transparency provisions.

Designate the labor inspectorate as the primary enforcement body for wage transparency obligations, with mandatory annual reporting to the Parliament and cooperation protocols with the Agency for Prevention of Corruption.
Invest in labor court capacity or establish mandatory mediation/conciliation procedures for work-life balance disputes to prevent new rights from being rendered meaningless by judicial backlog.

Conduct a regulatory impact assessment on the amendments' effect on SMEs, which comprise the majority of Montenegro's private sector, and consider phased implementation timelines for smaller enterprises.

Ensure the implementing regulations specify the compensation level for paternity leave (Directive 2019/1158 requires at least sick-pay level) and clarify whether the 10-day paternity leave is paid or unpaid.
Adopt a dedicated awareness campaign in partnership with the Ombudsman's office to ensure workers, particularly in the informal economy, are informed of their new rights.

counter_proposal.title: Rights With Teeth: An Enforcement-First Labor Reform
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Justice would retain all substantive rights in the current amendments but embed them within a comprehensive enforcement architecture. Rather than adopting rights first and addressing enforcement later, we would introduce the new entitlements alongside dedicated judicial and administrative mechanisms to ensure they are immediately actionable from day one of entry into force.
key_changes: Bundle the amendments with a parallel amendment to the Law on Inspections strengthening labor inspector powers to issue binding orders and progressive fines for work-life balance violations.
Introduce an expedited labor dispute procedure (maximum 90-day resolution) for claims involving denial of paternity leave, flexible work requests, or retaliation for exercising new rights.

Add an explicit reversed burden-of-proof clause: once an employee demonstrates a prima facie case that adverse treatment followed exercise of a protected right, the employer must prove the treatment was unrelated.
Establish an independent Wage Transparency Monitoring Unit within the labor inspectorate, with power to order compliance audits and publish anonymized findings annually.

Include a phased implementation schedule: immediate effect for public sector employers, 6-month transition for enterprises with 50+ employees, and 12-month transition for SMEs with fewer than 50 employees, accompanied by free compliance support.
Correct the directive citation and add a transposition table mapping each amendment provision to the specific article of the EU directive it transposes, ensuring full traceability for EU screening.
expected_benefits: Immediate enforceability of new rights prevents the common pattern of progressive legislation with no practical impact.
Reversed burden of proof deters employer retaliation, which is the primary barrier to employees exercising work-life balance rights in Montenegro.
Expedited labor dispute procedures reduce pressure on the already overburdened general court system.
Phased implementation gives SMEs time to adapt while maintaining accountability for larger employers and the public sector.
Transposition table demonstrates systematic EU alignment to the European Commission, supporting Montenegro's Chapter 23 closing benchmarks.
Wage transparency monitoring creates a factual basis for anti-discrimination enforcement and reduces arbitrary pay practices.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The substantive rights are already drafted; this counter-proposal adds procedural and institutional mechanisms that can be incorporated during parliamentary committee review without requiring a new legislative cycle. The expedited labor dispute procedure can be modeled on existing summary proceedings in the Code of Civil Procedure. The phased implementation schedule is standard practice in EU member states. The main resource requirement is staffing for the Wage Transparency Monitoring Unit, estimated at 5-8 additional inspectors, which is modest relative to the reform's ambition.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Prava sa zubima: Radna reforma s prioritetom izvršenja
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo pravde zadržalo bi sva materijalna prava iz trenutnih amandmana, ali bi ih ugradilo u sveobuhvatnu arhitekturu izvršenja. Umjesto da se prava najprije usvoje, a izvršenje razmotri naknadno, nova ovlaštenja uvela bi se zajedno s namjenskim sudskim i administrativnim mehanizmima kako bi bila neposredno primjenjiva od prvog dana stupanja na snagu.
key_changes_mne: Spojiti amandmane s paralelnim amandmanom na Zakon o inspekcijama, kojim se jačaju ovlašćenja inspektora rada za izdavanje obavezujućih naloga i progresivnih kazni za kršenje prava na ravnotežu između poslovnog i privatnog života.

Uvesti ubrzani postupak rješavanja radnih sporova (maksimalni rok od 90 dana) za zahtjeve koji uključuju uskraćivanje roditeljskog odsustva za očeve, zahtjeve za fleksibilno radno vrijeme ili odmazdu zbog korišćenja novih prava.

Dodati izričitu odredbu o obrnutom teretu dokazivanja: kada radnik učini vjerovatnim da je nepovoljni tretman uslijedio nakon što je iskoristio zaštićeno pravo, poslodavac mora dokazati da taj tretman nije bio s tim povezan.

Uspostaviti nezavisnu Jedinicu za praćenje transparentnosti plata u okviru Uprave za inspekcijske poslove, s ovlašćenjem za nalaganje revizija usklađenosti i godišnje objavljivanje anonimizovanih nalaza.

Uključiti fazni raspored primjene: neposredno dejstvo za poslodavce u javnom sektoru, prelazni period od 6 mjeseci za privredna društva s 50 ili više zaposlenih i prelazni period od 12 mjeseci za mala i srednja preduzeća s manje od 50 zaposlenih, uz besplatnu podršku pri usklađivanju.
Ispraviti navod direktive i dodati tabelu transpozicije koja svaki amandman vezuje za konkretan član direktive EU čiju transpoziciju vrši, čime se osigurava potpuna sljedivost za skrining EU.
expected_benefits_mne: Neposredna izvršivost novih prava sprječava uobičajeni obrazac donošenja progresivnih propisa bez stvarnog praktičnog učinka.
Obrnuti teret dokazivanja odvraća poslodavce od odmazde, koja je glavna prepreka da radnici u Crnoj Gori koriste prava na ravnotežu između poslovnog i privatnog života.
Ubrzani postupci rješavanja radnih sporova smanjuju pritisak na ionako preopterećeni sistem opštih sudova.
Fazna primjena daje malim i srednjim preduzećima vremena da se prilagode, uz istovremeno očuvanje odgovornosti za veće poslodavce i javni sektor.
Tabela transpozicije dokazuje sistematsku usklađenost s EU pred Evropskom komisijom i podržava ispunjavanje mjerila za zatvaranje Poglavlja 23.
Praćenje transparentnosti plata stvara činjeničnu osnovu za primjenu antidiskriminacionih mjera i smanjuje samovoljne prakse u pogledu zarada.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Materijalna prava su već formulisana; ovim kontra-prijedlogom dodaju se proceduralni i institucionalni mehanizmi koji se mogu ugraditi tokom razmatranja u parlamentarnom odboru, bez potrebe za pokretanjem novog zakonodavnog ciklusa. Ubrzani postupak rješavanja radnih sporova može se modelovati prema postojećim skraćenim postupcima iz Zakonika o građanskom postupku. Fazni raspored primjene standardna je praksa u državama članicama EU. Glavni resursni zahtjev odnosi se na popunjavanje radnih mjesta u Jedinici za praćenje transparentnosti plata — procjenjuje se da je potrebno 5 do 8 dodatnih inspektora, što je skromno u odnosu na ambiciju reforme.

assessments.summary_mne: The Labor Law amendments represent a constitutionally sound and EU-aligned reform package that advances fundamental rights protections, particularly gender equality and work-life balance. The transposition of EU Directives 2019/1158 and 2019/1152 strengthens Montenegro's Chapter 23 credentials on fundamental rights and signals genuine legislative progress on the EU accession path. However, the Ministry of Justice identifies significant concerns regarding enforceability, the absence of robust judicial remedy mechanisms for new rights, and an apparent legislative reference error (citing "Directive 2019/820" rather than 2019/1158), which must be corrected before parliamentary adoption to ensure legal certainty.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: The amendments are a positive step for fundamental rights and EU alignment, correctly transposing key work-life balance and transparent working conditions directives. The top concerns are weak enforcement infrastructure (Montenegro's labor inspectorate rarely initiates sanctions), missing judicial remedy provisions for employees whose new rights are denied, and a legislative citation error that could create legal uncertainty. The Ministry of Justice recommends coupling these substantive rights with explicit enforcement mechanisms, including dedicated labor court procedures and strengthened inspection powers.
key_concerns_mne: The decision text cites 'EU Directive 2019/820' which does not exist; this is likely a reference to Directive 2019/1158 and must be corrected before parliamentary adoption to ensure legal certainty and proper transposition documentation.

Montenegro's labor inspectorate has a documented pattern of passive enforcement, rarely initiating sanctions despite having authority to do so, which threatens the practical effectiveness of the new employee rights.
